text:
2017 BCSC 309 Price v. 481530 B.C. Ltd.
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF BRITISH COLUMBIA
Citation:
Price v. 481530 B.C. Ltd.,
2017 BCSC 309
Date: 20170224
Docket: S149212
Registry: Vancouver
Between:
Michael James Price
Plaintiff
And
481530 B.C. Ltd. doing business as Micron Laboratories,
8577935 Canada Corporation, Farrukh David Robson,
Sabrina Yasmeen Robson,
Katrina Robson also known as Sheena Robson
and Jonathan Robson
Defendants
Before: The Honourable Madam Justice Burke
Reasons for Judgment
Counsel for the Plaintiff:
A.H. Narod
Counsel for the Defendants:
G. Allen
Place and Date of Hearing:
Vancouver, B.C.
January 20 and 27, 2017
Place and Date of Judgment:
Vancouver, B.C.
February 24, 2017
INTRODUCTION
[1] This is an application to settle an order relating to my decision in Price v. 481530 B.C. Ltd., 2016 BCSC 1940.
BACKGROUND
[2] The plaintiff, Mr. Price, worked for Micron Laboratories for nearly two decades until he was wrongly dismissed. The circumstances surrounding his dismissal were particularly difficult, involving tortious conduct on behalf of the personal respondents. In my Reasons, I awarded general damages of 20 months' compensation in lieu of reasonable notice, based on Mr. Price's average monthly salary and commission.
[3] The plaintiff now seeks clarification as to whether the general damages award was inclusive of lost employment benefits. Mr. Price maintains damages for unpaid vacation pay up to the date of his termination from employment and for the loss of the leased car benefit provided by Micron Laboratories during the notice period should be included in that award.
ANALYSIS
1. Entitlement
i. Vacation Pay
[4] The plaintiff says that employees terminated from employment are entitled to compensation for unpaid, unused vacation pay accrued to the date of termination, unless their employment contract contains a contrary term. He says that as he was wrongfully dismissed and his employment contract contained no contrary term, he is entitled to outstanding vacation pay.
[5] I agree Mr. Price is entitled to outstanding vacation pay.
[6] The Employment Standards Act, R.S.B.C. 1996, c. 113, makes clear that the plaintiff, as an employee, is entitled to unpaid vacation pay on termination. Sections 58(1) and (3) explicitly provide that an employer must pay an employee vacation pay in the amounts stated and that any vacation pay an employee is entitled to when the employment terminates must be paid to the employee at the time when wages are paid.
[7] The jurisprudence is also clear that the plaintiff, as a dismissed employee, is entitled to unpaid vacation pay to the date of termination. In Liboiron v. IBM Canada Ltd., 2015 BCSC 1523, Madam Justice Harris unequivocally stated at para. 46: "The jurisprudence in British Columbia has established that an employee is entitled to any vacation pay accrued before the date of termination".
[8] I further note that there is no evidence that Mr. Price's employment contract with his employer, Micron Laboratories, which was oral in nature, contained a contrary term denying him entitlement to vacation pay.
[9] The respondents nonetheless maintain that a plaintiff seeking damages for unpaid vacation pay must provide evidence as to what vacation was taken and what vacation entitlement remains outstanding. They say Mr. Price did not adduce any evidence in this regard and therefore his claim must fail.
[10] To the contrary, I conclude there is sufficient evidence to ground the plaintiff's claim: Pritchard v. The Stuffed Animal House Ltd., 2010 BCSC 213 at paras. 58-65. Mr. Price testified at trial that he worked every business day and worked long hours but did not take vacations. There was also evidence employees at Micron Laboratories were entitled to vacation pay but the plaintiff received none. Indeed, Mr. David Robson conceded in cross-examination that Mr. Price was entitled to vacation pay and that he did not remember whether Mr. Price paid himself vacation pay, which is not inconsistent with the plaintiff's position.
[11] The respondents point to evidence that Mr. Price took trips during his employment to Palm Springs, Great Wolf Lodge and Phoenix, and that he termed expenses covered by the company credit card while away as "personal" and reimbursed the company for them. Yet as Mr. Price explained, those trips were taken as non-compensatory time off, as compensation for much unpaid overtime, rather than as vacation days. Indeed, he said he would even work while on those trips and at least one of the trips was to seek assistance for his disabled son.
[12] The respondents further argue that the plaintiff's notice of civil claim did not claim vacation pay and that that claim should not be allowed now. However, paragraph 42(a) of the notice of civil claim reads: "The Plaintiff has suffered damage and injury, including: (a) loss of salary, commission and benefits". Although it could have been clearer, I find that this statement about loss of "benefits" was sufficiently clear to ground the present claim.
[13] They alternatively argue the plaintiff made no reference at trial to vacation pay in his closing submissions and therefore they were entitled to assume the claim had been abandoned. They say the only reference to the claim for vacation pay was made in two vague charts provided by the plaintiff entitled "Damages for Breach of Contract". While the argument was voluminous, dealing with many issues in a very difficult case, there was sufficient reference to maintain the claim. Accordingly, it cannot be said to be abandoned.
ii. Vehicle Benefit
[14] The plaintiff maintains that a wrongfully dismissed employee who was provided with a leased vehicle is entitled to compensation for the loss of that benefit during the notice period. As a wrongfully dismissed employee, he claims damages for the loss of the leased Mercedes provided by Micron Laboratories.
[15] Mr. Price is entitled to compensation for the loss of the vehicle benefit.
[16] The jurisprudence is clear wrongfully dismissed employees, like Mr. Price, can be compensated for the loss of the personal benefit of a company car: Hawkes v. Levelton Holdings Ltd., 2012 BCSC 1219.
[17] Further, I find it would be appropriate to compensate the plaintiff in this case. The purpose of damages in this context is to place the plaintiff in the same position he would have been in if he had been given proper notice: Hawkes at para. 34. Applying that principle, if Mr. Price had been given proper notice, he would have had personal use of the company vehicle for an additional 20 months. Compensation is appropriate to place him in the same position he would have been in if he had been given that benefit.
[18] The respondents object, saying that a plaintiff seeking damages for the personal benefit associated with a car allowance must adduce evidence of how much of the allowance could be attributable to personal use, from which the court can calculate the value of the personal benefit. They say the plaintiff has adduced no evidence in this regard.
[19] However, in Hawkes the Court found that a total absence of evidence as to how much of the plaintiff's vehicle benefit could be attributable to personal use was not fatal to a claim for a car allowance: at para. 244.
[20] In any case, I find that there was sufficient evidence on this matter. Mr. Price testified that Micron Laboratories leased a vehicle in its name for his use which he in fact used for business and personal use. He said there were never any conditions placed upon the use of the vehicle, restricting its use for business purposes or having to leave the vehicle at work, which he did not. I am able to infer that Mr. Price mostly had unrestricted access to the company vehicle for personal use whenever he was not at work or conducting business.
2. Amounts
i. Vacation Pay
[21] The plaintiff says he is entitled to damages for 5 years of accrued vacation pay at 4% of his remuneration and 14 years at 6%.
[22] I agree with those figures. Section 58(1) of the Employment Standards Act sets out the rates of vacation pay that employees are entitled to:
Vacation pay
58 (1) An employer must pay an employee the following amount of vacation pay:
(a) after 5 calendar days of employment, at least 4% of the employee's total wages during the year of employment entitling the employee to the vacation pay;
(b) after 5 consecutive years of employment, at least 6% of the employee's total wages during the year of employment entitling the employee to the vacation pay.
As Mr. Price was employed for 19 consecutive years, from 1994 to 2013, he is clearly entitled to 4% of his total wages for the first 5 years and 6% for the following 14 years.
[23] The more contentious question is how to calculate the plaintiff's total wages for those years. Section 1 of the Employment Standards Act defines "wages" as including: "salaries, commissions or money, paid or payable by an employer to an employee for work". Hence, Mr. Price's total wages include his base salary and commission.
[24] The respondents submit that the plaintiff adduced no evidence of his income for each of the 19 years of his employment. While I agree that the evidence is somewhat lacking and unclear at times, he testified at trial and provided documentation about his salary and commission. He said his initial salary was $350 per week, increasing to $450 in the late 1990s and $550 more recently, although the precise dates are unclear. In addition, he said he would earn a specific percentage of all monthly gross sales as commission, assuming the company made at least $15,000 in monthly gross sales, later rising to $18,000.
[25] The plaintiff asks me to use the same income as fixed in my Reasons, based on his base salary of $550 per week and average monthly commission. But as reflected in the evidence his base salary was less in the past, only $350 to $450 per week. Section 58 of the Employment Standards Act requires that vacation pay must be based on the employee's income for each year that vacation pay is being claimed. It would therefore be inaccurate to use that fixed income.
[26] I calculate his total wages as follows. His estimated base annual salary for the first five years was $18,200 ($350 x 52 weeks), $23,400 for the next 10 years ($450 x 52 weeks), and $28,600 for the final four years ($550 x 52 weeks), finding on his average monthly commission for the years 2010, 2011 and 2012 can be used; $51,360 annually ($4,280 x 12 months). Hence, Mr. Price is entitled to 4% of $347,800 ($18,200 + $51,360 x 5 years) and 6% of $1,067,440 (($23,400 + $51,360 x 10 years) + ($28,600 + $51,360 x 4 years)), or $13,912 and $64,046.40 respectively.
[27] The respondents objected to Mr. Price's testimony that he took no vacations in 19 years of employment as simply not credible. Indeed, they say Mr. Price conceded that he took vacations, albeit never longer than a week.
[28] I found Mr. Price to be a credible witness overall; I do, however, have some concerns with this position. A fair approach therefore is to make a deduction in damages to account for the possibility that he took some vacations. Even if he did take some days off, I have no doubt a far greater surplus in unpaid vacation pay would remain. Hence, I deduct 5%, reducing the total amount of vacation pay to $74,060.48 (($13,912 - $695.60) + ($64,046.40 - $3,202.32)).
[29] The respondents further argue that the plaintiff should be held to the original amount claimed. During closing submissions at trial, the plaintiff provided a chart entitled "Damages for Breach of Contract", which calculated his entitlement to vacation pay at $14,425.20. They say it is unfair for the plaintiff to greatly increase his claim long after final submissions were made and that he should be held to his original figure.
[30] I am not persuaded by this argument. As the respondents themselves noted, in their objection to Mr. Price's entitlement to vacation pay, the matter of vacation pay was not fully argued at trial during closing submissions. Both parties, however, were notified and given an opportunity to address the issue more thoroughly through this application. It would not be fair to hold the plaintiff to an earlier figure and deny him the benefit of the more thorough submissions.
ii. Vehicle Benefit
[31] The plaintiff claims that he is entitled to compensation for paying the cost of the leased vehicle personally for seven months and the value of his personal use of the leased vehicle for the remaining 13 months of the notice period.
[32] The respondents acknowledge that Mr. Price is entitled to be reimbursed for the lease payments.
[33] I accept that admission. When the corporate account was drained, Mr. Price was forced to pay the cost of the lease himself for seven months until the company vehicle could be returned. The monthly cost of leasing the vehicle, a Mercedes, was $714.73. I find that Mr. Price is entitled to be compensated $5,003.11 ($714.73 x 7 months) for the loss for the period of February 10, 2014 through to September 9, 2014.
[34] The next issue is determining the value of the personal use of the leased vehicle. Hawkes provides some guidance. The plaintiff in that case, Mr. Hawkes, was provided with a car allowance in lieu of a leased company vehicle until his wrongful dismissal. The allowance went towards a vehicle which he used for business and personal use. The Court found there was no evidence as to how much of the allowance could be attributable to personal use. It nonetheless inferred that 50% of the vehicle benefit was personally related, which amounted to half of the vehicle allowance.
[35] The circumstances in Hawkes are similar to the present case. As with Mr. Hawkes, Mr. Price was given a vehicle benefit, for business and personal use, prior to his wrongful dismissal. Further, both plaintiffs seem to have had unrestricted personal use of their vehicles in their free time. In light of these similarities, I have decided to follow Hawkes and infer that Mr. Price also had personal use of his vehicle for 50% of the time. Accordingly, the value of the personal use of the leased vehicle is $357.37 per month ($714.73 / 2), for a total of $1,786.85 ($357.37 x 5 months); being the five months in the notice period he was without a vehicle.
CONCLUSION
[36] I conclude that damages for unpaid vacation pay up to the date of his termination from employment and loss of the leased car benefit provided by Micron Laboratories during the notice period should be included in the award owing from my judgment of October 24, 2016. Mr. Price is entitled to the amounts of $74,060.48 for outstanding vacation pay and $6,789.96 for the lost vehicle benefit. He is also entitled to pre-judgment interest on these amounts.
[37] I note in the event the parties are unable to agree on costs, either party may file a request to speak to costs within three months of the date of these Reasons.
The Honourable Madam Justice E. Burke
